preamble: The shift from the voiced labio-velar approximant [w] to the voiced labiodental fricative [v] is a widespread fortition process in the history of Indo-European languages. It involves the loss of the velar component and the strengthening of the labial constriction from an approximant to a fricative.

examples.note: Original [w] fortified to [v] by the Middle High German period.
Classical Latin [w] became a bilabial fricative [β] and eventually a labiodental fricative [v] in most Romance varieties.

sources: Lass, R. (1997). Historical Linguistics and Language Change. Cambridge University Press.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
